title: Roast Pork Belly
subtitle: Slow-cooked belly with blistered crackling and apple sauce
excerpt: Pork belly is the most forgiving cut in the British roasting repertoire. Slow-cooked at 160°C for three hours, the thick fat layers render into the meat, making it almost impossible to dry out. A final blast at 240°C crisps the skin to proper crackling. It is richer than loin and needs only apple sauce and watercress alongside.
sourceNotes: Traditional British pork belly roasting method. Nigel Slater, The Kitchen Diaries (2005): slow-cooked belly with apple. Traditional British Sunday roast practice using the less fashionable belly cut.

content.text: Pork belly was for a long time the cheap cut that no one roasted for Sunday lunch. That changed around the early 2000s when restaurant chefs began slow-cooking it as the centrepiece of a plate. At home, the method is simpler: three hours at 160°C to
the deep fat layers, then a short blast at 240°C to blister the scored skin into crackling. The slow phase is almost entirely unattended.

A bone-in belly is traditional and holds its shape better during the long cook, but boneless (rolled and tied) is easier to carve. The skin must be scored deeply and dried overnight; the rest follows from the oven.

items.name: Pork belly
items.prepNote: bone-in or boneless, rind on, scored

content.text: A rack in the roasting pan keeps the belly lifted above the rendered fat. Without the rack the skin can soften in its own drippings during the slow phase.

Score the rind at 1 cm intervals, cutting through skin and fat to the meat. Rub {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt thoroughly into the scores. Season the meat side with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Set rind-side up on a rack and refrigerate uncovered overnight. The skin must be completely dry before the blast phase.

Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Place the halved {{onion}} onions and crushed {{garlic}} of garlic cloves in the base of the roasting pan. Set the rack over them and place the belly rind-side up. Pour {{dry-cider}} of cider into the pan base if using. Roast for 3 hours. The fat should be partially rendered and the skin beginning to colour.

Raise the oven to 240°C (220°C fan). Return the belly for 20 to 25 minutes until the skin is blistered and crackling all over. Watch it closely: the line between fully blistered crackling and burning is narrow at this temperature.

Rest for 15 minutes. Carve between the ribs if bone-in, or slice across into thick pieces if boneless. The crackling can be scored further with the carving knife or broken into pieces with the back of a spoon.

rows.fix: Give the oven 20 minutes to reach 240°C. Pat the skin dry before the blast. If still soft after 25 minutes, raise to 250°C for a final 10 minutes.
rows.cause: Skin not dry enough, or blast phase temperature not reached
rows.symptom: Crackling not blistering during the blast phase

rows.fix: A lean belly does not benefit from the slow method as much as a fatty one. Check the butcher's description before buying.
rows.cause: Belly was very lean with thin fat layers, or the rack sat in too much liquid
rows.symptom: Meat dry despite the long slow cook

rows.fix: Empty the roasting pan of accumulated fat midway through the slow phase. Use a roasting rack to keep the belly above the drippings.
rows.cause: Excess fat dripping onto the oven floor
rows.symptom: Too much smoke from rendered fat in oven

content.text: Pork belly has always sat at the working-class end of the butcher's counter in Britain, where its high fat content made it inexpensive and deeply sustaining. It appears in Mrs Beeton as a cut for braising rather than roasting, and for most of the twentieth century it remained a background ingredient for bacon and lard rather than a Sunday joint. Its rehabilitation as a prestige cut happened largely in restaurant kitchens and was well established by 2010. Serve with apple sauce, roast potatoes, and wilted greens.
